go:10905.title: GATE CSE 1989 | Question: 1-vii, ISRO2015-14
go:10905.question:
A hash table with ten buckets with one slot per bucket is shown in the following figure. The symbols $S1$ to $S7$ initially entered using a hashing function with linear probing. The maximum number of comparisons needed in searching an item that is not present is

  1. $4$
  2. $5$
  3. $6$
  4. $3$

go:87095.title: GATE CSE 1989 | Question: 3-i
go:87095.question:
An unrestricted use of the "go to" statement is harmful because of which of the following reason (s):

  1. It makes it more difficult to verify programs.
  2. It makes programs more inefficient.
  3. It makes it more difficult to modify existing programs.
  4. It results in the compiler generating longer machine code.

go:87117.title: GATE CSE 1989 | Question: 3-ii
go:87117.question:
Context-free languages and regular languages are both closed under the operation (s) of :

  1. Union
  2. Intersection
  3. Concatenation
  4. Complementation

go:87123.title: GATE CSE 1989 | Question: 3-iii
go:87123.question:
Which of the following problems are undecidable?

  1. Membership problem in context-free languages.
  2. Whether a given context-free language is regular.
  3. Whether a finite state automation halts on all inputs.
  4. Membership problem for type $0$ languages.

go:87143.title: GATE CSE 1989 | Question: 3-ixa
go:87143.question:
Which one of the following statements (s) is/are FALSE?

  1. Overlaying is used to run a program, which is longer than the address space of the computer.
  2. Optimal binary search tree construction can be performed efficiently by using dynamic programming.
  3. Depth first search cannot be used to find connected components of a graph.
  4. Given the prefix and postfix walls over a binary tree, the binary tree can be uniquely constructed.

go:87126.title: GATE CSE 1989 | Question: 3-v
go:87126.question:
Which of the following well-formed formulas are equivalent?

  1. $P \rightarrow Q$
  2. $\neg Q \rightarrow \neg P$
  3. $\neg P \vee Q$
  4. $\neg Q \rightarrow P$

go:37264.title: GATE CSE 1989 | Question: 3-viii
go:37264.question:
In which of the following case(s) is it possible to obtain different results for call-by-reference and call-by-name parameter passing?

  1. Passing an expression as a parameter
  2. Passing an array as a parameter
  3. Passing a pointer as a parameter
  4. Passing as array element as a parameter

go:93172.title: GATE CSE 1989 | Question: 13a
go:93172.question: A language uses an alphabet of six letters, $\left\{a, b, c, d, e, f\right\}$. The relative frequency of use of each letter of the alphabet in the language is as given below: $$\begin{array}{c|c}\hline \text{LETTER} & \text{RELATIVE FREQUENCY OF USE}\\\hline a & 0.19  \\ b & 0.05  \\ c & 0.17  \\ d & 0.08  \\ e & 0.40 \\ f & 0.11  \\\hline \end{array}$$Design a prefix binary code for the language which would minimize the average length of the encoded words of the language.
